yaml.title: Problem Set 2 - Solutions
yaml.subtitle:
Economics 100B - Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
Prof. Muehlegger
yaml.author: Prof. Muehlegger

markdown.markdown:
Question 1: Monopoly and Price Caps
A monopoly faces an inverse demand curve given by P = 240 – 2Q, and has total costs given by TC = Q 2 +
2400.

(a) What is the profit maximizing quantity and price for the monopoly?

MR = 240 – 4Q, MC = 2Q

MR = MC implies 240-4Q = 2Q -> Q = 40 -> P = 240-2(40) = 160.

(b) What are the monopolist’s profits?

Using the price and quantity from part (a), we have Profit = TR – TC = 160*40 – 40 2 – 2400 = 6400 – 1600
– 2400 = 2400.

(c) Draw the diagram clearly labeling the inverse demand, marginal cost and marginal revenue
curves, and shading the appropriate regions corresponding to deadweight loss, consumer and
producer surplus.

                                           MC
        240

                CS
        160
                                       DWL
                PS
        80

                            MR           D

                      40 60              120

(d) Calculate the deadweight loss, consumer and producer surplus.

CS = 1/2 *80*40 = 1600

PS = 1/2 *40*80+40*80 = 1600 + 3200 = 4800

DWL = 1/2*20*80 = 800

(e) What is the price cap / price ceiling the government would set that would maximize welfare?

The price cap that would maximize welfare would be the price that would equate MC and demand. At that
point, the societal value of the last unit that was consumed (i.e., the height of the demand curve) is equal to
the societal cost of the last unit that was produced (i.e., the height of MC).

Equating D and MC, we have: 240 – 2Q = 2Q -> Q = 60 -> Pcap = 120

(f) Suppose that the government regulates the monopoly under the price cap / price ceiling in the
previous part. What is the monopolist’s profits under the price cap?

The monopolist’s profits under the price cap are given by:

Profit = TR – TC = 120*60 – 60*60 – 2400 = 7200 – 3600 – 2400 = 1200.

Question 2: Price Discrimination

Suppose a monopolist sells goods to two types of customers: (1) business travelers, and (2) leisure
travelers, with inverse demand curves as follows:

Business inverse demand curve: P = 500 – 4Q

Leisure inverse demand curve: P = 300 – Q

The marginal cost to rent a car is constant and equal to 100.

(a) If the firm can perfectly price discriminate, calculate firm profits. What is consumer surplus and
deadweight loss?

If the firm can perfectly price discriminate, the firm will set a price for each customer equal to their
willingness to pay and sell to all customers with willingness to pay above MC. Graphically, the firm’s
profits will be given by sum of the two areas in gold in the diagram below.

            Business customers                                        Leisure customers

      500

                                                                300

      100                                                       100

                   100 125                                                     200    300

Mathematically,

Profits = 1/2*400*100 + 1/2 *200 *200 = 20000+20000 = 40000.

Perfect price discrimination leaves no surplus for consumers, nor does it create deadweight loss.

(b) Suppose the firm can set one price for business customers and a different price for leisure
customers. What prices and quantities for each group maximize the monopolist’s profits?

In this case, the firm sets MR = MC for each group to determine the profit maximizing Q and P for
each group.

Business customers: MR = MC -> 500-8Q = 100 -> Q = 50 -> P = 300.

Leisure customers: MR = MC -> 300 – 2Q = 100 -> Q = 100 -> P = 200.

Profits = TR – TC = 50*300 + 100*200 – 100*(50+100) = 15000 + 20000 – 15000 = 20000.

(c) Are consumer surplus, producer surplus and welfare higher or lower in part (b) and part (a).
Briefly explain why.

Producer surplus is lower in (b) than (a), since under perfect price discrimination, the firm can
capture the entirety of surplus.

Consumer surplus is higher in (b) and (a), since under perfect price discrimination, the firm can
capture all of consumer surplus, when it sets prices equal to each customers willingness to pay.

Welfare is lower in part (b) than (a). Under perfect price discrimination, there is no deadweight loss.
But under group price discrimination, the firm cuts quantity to drive up price to each group.

(d) What three things have to be true for a firm to be able to price discriminate?

The firm: (1) needs the ability to set prices above marginal cost, (2) needs the ability to differentiate
consumers who have different willingness-to-pay, and (3) needs the ability to prevent (or
substantially limit) resale.

(e) Suppose now that the firm can only set one price for all customers (i.e., can’t price discriminate).
Will this make business customers better off? What about leisure customers? What about the firm?

As discussed at the end of last class, if the firm can’t price discriminate, it has two options: (1) focus on the
high valuation (business) customers, or (2) sell to both groups at a “middle-of-the-road” price. In both
cases, this lowers firm profits. If the firm can’t price discriminate, it makes the low-valuation (leisure)
costomers worse off, while making the business customers better off.
